turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument now in Number oh oh fifteen ninety-five, Hoffman Plastic Compounds, Inc. versus The National Labor Relations Board. Mr. McCortney.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. The issue in this case is whether the award of back pay to an undocumented alien who never was legally present or legally authorized to work in this country conflicts with this Court's holding in Sure-Tan or with this country's immigration laws and policies. In Sure-Tan, this Court held that the discriminatees in that case must be deemed unavailable for work and the accrual of back pay therefore tolled during any period when they were not lawfully entitled to be present and employed in the United States. The Sure-Tan Court's back pay limitation is consistent with the INA's dual requirement that to be employed in this country an alien must be both legally present and legally authorized to work. Despite this dual requirement, the board contends that Castro is entitled to back pay even though he never was legally present or legally authorized to work in this country during the back pay period. The fatal flaw in the board's position lies in its adherence to its physical availability doctrine. The board contends that this C- Court denied back pay to the discriminatees in Sure-Tan only because they had left the country and were physically unavailable for work. If the board is right, the Sure-Tan discriminatees could have illegally reentered the country to establish physical presence and to commence the accrual of back pay. However, such reentry would violate the immigration laws of this country and be contrary to this Court's holding in Sure-Tan.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Have the immigration... Well, didn't...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: laws changed at all in any re- relevant respects since our decision in Sure-Tan?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, the passage of IRCA in nineteen eighty-six.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: And how how w- what what relevant changes did IRCA make?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: IRCA was an amendment to the INA that made the employment, the knowing employment of un- undocumented aliens unauthorized from the employer's perspective.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And and is it correct that when Sure-Tan was argued, aricka [: IRCA] if that's the way you pronounce it, was being considered by Congress and the Government in its argument told us that if aricka [: IRCA] had been passed, back pay would not be available?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: country to claim back pay.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: conditioned it on legal presence, I mean, in fact they were not present.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So you have to acknowledge that it was dictum.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I I would I would not call it dicta only because the Court set forth the conditions upon which the discriminatees might receive back pay, if any, if they were to legally reenter...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the country.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: but but but it only had to say one condition in order to decide the case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and it...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: went on to say, and in addition you have to be available t- to work, but but that was really unnecessary to the decision.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Well, the threshold condition was legal reentry, and that was the starting point of the analysis.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: McCortney if I i-

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: If that is so, then why did the Court make the comment on page nine oh one of the opinion in footnote eleven, talking about the board has said that it's it will make it this arbitrary number of weeks, and the Court said the board has never intemp- attem- attempted to impose a minimum back pay award that the employer must pay regardless of the actual evidence as to such issues as the employee's availability to work th- All of that would have been unnecessary if it were just this blanket rule, right?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I think in footnote eleven what the Court was addressing was the minimum back pay award of six months that the court of appeals had imposed and the board had adopted, and then the board tried to defend that award by arguing that they had in other cases had estimates of back pay, despite the fact that the discriminatee was unable est- unable to establish with any certainty the probable length of the back pay period.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, if if the opinion in Sure-Tan we think equivocal, if it's a wash either way, what what is your principal argument for reversal here?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Assuming that Sure-Tan is equivocal?

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: That the board's award of back pay conflicts with the immigration laws and policies of this country as embodied in the INA and IRCA.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Well, what do you what do you say of the the argument that the board makes that even even if the the the the dictum in in Sure-Tan were a were a a holding as as you say it it should be treated, that the statute has changed the landscape, and that the board's rule in effect is is in aid of the statute, because the board is saying the illegality on the part of the employer comes when the employer knowingly employs these people despite knowledge of of their illegal character, so in effect we're going to say that the obligation to pay back pay stops when the employer knows that the alien in fact is illegally there.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Your Honor, the just the mere prospect of receiving back pay in this case encouraged Castro to extend his illegal stay by more than four years and to continue...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: using...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: I I think I think you're right, but the board is saying, yeah, there's that inducement on one side, but there's a very powerful employer inducement if we come out the other way, and although I don't think the board put it in these words, I take it the board is saying, we think the employers are going to make a rational decision about inducements more clearly or more obviously than employees, the illegal aliens might do, so we're going to prefer the the inducement or the policy that is going to have the strongest effect on the employers.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: But the board is saying we can't have it both ways.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Y- Your Honor, I don't believe that those ind- the the deterrence of the employer to that knowingly hires illegal aliens or employs them, that awarding back pay is necessary in light of the the sanctions under IRCA that now subject employers that do so to civil and criminal sanctions, and so...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, would- wouldn't it also be the case that that if the employer, by reason of this inducement, hired su- supposed inducement hired somebody whom he knew at the time of the hiring to be an unlawful, an illegal alien, even applying the board's rule, he wouldn't have to pay any back pay.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What i- what is th- here I I think Justice Souter is we said in our cases just what Justice Souter said, I take it, INS V Lopez-Mendoza, you know quoting what Sure-Tan says.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And if this...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: doesn't, what would?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Your Honor, what it doesn't address is the violation of the INA.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Your Honor, I think what the sentent- sentence means, at least my reading of it, is that retrospective sanctions may be imposed, but not necessarily,

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Fine.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: What other what other ones are there, is is is what what other retrospective sanctions are there?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Can the board fine can the board fine the employer?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What else can the board do to punish the employer for for the unfair labor...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: practice?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: and desist order, as in this case, with the threat of contempt sanctions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's a that's a prospective...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: sanction, isn't it?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So you really don't have any explanation for that for that language .

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: All I would say, Your Honor,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: is that...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I I suppose your explanation is that that's as much dictum as as as your your opponent says Sure-Tan was.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So it's a wash, right?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I take it that Sure-Tan was dealing with a case where the employer did know in respect to the sanction that the employee, the alien, was illegal so Sure-Tan did not consider the kind of sanction here at issue, which is a sanction that applies only to the period where the employer did not know that the employee was an illegal, hence the employer was not violating the immigration law.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: It still doesn't address, Your Honor, the fact that the illegal presence and the use of fraudulent documents and working without authorization is a violation of the INA.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Irres-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's the question I was going to ask you.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well what now I I...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I know I know the use of the...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: green card is, but is the is the working and getting pay unlawful under the immigration laws?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Yes, it is.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I thought...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: I thought IRCA itself enacted a provision making it illegal for aliens to provide false documentation to get employment.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Yes, it was, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: eight USC eleven...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: did IRCA add provisions and impose obligations on prospective employers to require them to review the documents and make sure that they appear to be regular on their face?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And that was a new provision?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: That was a new provision.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And is there any allegation in this case that the employer failed to comply with that?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I- It's i-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: May I ask if...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: your position would apply if this were a violation of the Fair Labor Standards Act instead of the m- labor act? If the employer had underpaid the employee, would he have a right to legal back pay?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Your Honor, I in the amicus brief of the States they seem to equate back pay under the NLRA with back pay under the FLSA when they're two different things.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Back pay under...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: they're two different I just want to know what your position is on that.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: No, we would not advocate at all, and we have not, taking wages away from undocumented aliens that have been earned for work already performed.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Even though they it it was a crime to do any work? Why i- if if you're s- sticking with your theory that everything that this person did on that job, from presenting the false documentation on, was unlawful, so why should he be paid anything for unlawful activity? You're making a distinction between the Fair Labor Standards Act and the NLRA, but your theory, I think, would cover both.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, we give certain rights and benefits to undocumented aliens that are already in this country, and we do that to level the playing field between undocumented aliens and American workers so that unscrupulous employers won't prefer undocumented aliens over American workers.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, furthermore, just from the standpoint of equity, I suppose the employer has benefitted from the services in in in the case that Justice Ginsburg puts,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and there's there's there's no...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: benefit here save, arguably, from the from illegal lab- labor practice, but there's there's no benefit for work received.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: That's that's absolutely correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: In fact th-

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: would you clarify one thing for me?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Do I understand that if the employer and the employee both know of the illegality, that then there's no back pay?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I mean it seems to me that's absolutely upside down.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Well, Your...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Honor s-

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: the and of course, you don't this is the Government's position, you don't have to defend it, but in in other words, as I understand the Government says once the employer knows that there's a violation and continues it, then he's no longer liable for back pay.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, that's the problem with the rule, is that it in some ways rewards the unscrupulous employer in Sure-Tan and penalizes the innocent pl- employer, as in Hoffman.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: get him deported, and cut off back pay.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: difference is, the of course, that if the employer knowingly hires this alien, he's subject to a lot of other penalties under the immigration law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: who, you know, all he does, he says, I've checked their cards, I've checked their cards, the cards say they're here legally, and he runs some God-awful sweatshop.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, he has to pay for the sweat, though, doesn't he?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Abolutely.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: and it's pretty low cost, because he's violating every labor law under the sun.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, i- if, indeed, the worker, u- u- under this scheme you're proposing, there's no doubt that if the document i- undocumented alien works, he's going to get paid for the work.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Absolutely, Your Honor, no doubt, and we have...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: never advocated...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: there's an OSHA violation.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Could be.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: What about title seven?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Under title seven, i- th- if it's back pay exactly like back pay under the National Labor Relations Act, where it's unearned wages for work not performed during the back pay period, then that would be a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: problem.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: this the allegation is, they kept me in this entry-level job, although I was qualified for the next steps, because I was a woman and they never promote women.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: In that situation, Your Honor, if it were discovered that that the u- illegal that this person was an illegal alien...

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Make it just like this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Then there are other remedies...

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: available under title seven to effectuate the policies of the act and to enforce compliance.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: I'm asking about back pay for title seven.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: It would be bracketed if in your situation, you would get back pay, and let me explain the difference in this case.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: So in title seven, if she were laid off, say, because they laid off all the women before they la- laid off any men, so sh- she would also have a duty to mitigate in those circumstances, would the result be different?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: this country.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: complaint, if it were if it were complete, should should read something like, you know, I shouldn't have been working at all, and it was illegal for me to be working at all, and I'm complaining because I only got twelve thousand dollars in illegal illegal wages.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I don't.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: But you just told me that you would bracket the title seven with the FLSA.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: I Yes, because I notwithstanding Justice Scalia's very good example that I don't find that appealing, that there is a way that this Court can distinguish between the National Labor Relations Act, which is remedial in nature, and all these other State and Federal discrimination laws that have punitive features to it that are not dependent on the undocumented alien's ability to work in this country.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Well, I don't have any doubt that there are other ways for us to distinguish this, and it may very well be that if this were an original matter with us we wouldn't have sort of struck the balance where the board did, but you've got the element here, the added element here of of the board's decision, and I suppose we owe some kind of respect to it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, Your HonorIf...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: I could just supplement that, don- do we owe any deference to the views of the Attorney General of the United States, who is responsible for enforcing these a- both statutes?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Your Honor, I don't believe so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: We don't give a deference...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to administrative agencies as to what damages are available in court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: extraordinary.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: were there agency adjudications here?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: In what way, Your Honor? There was...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: there was a...

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: back pay hearing, there was a underlying unfair labor practice proceeding.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Which and and the result of which was?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: That the employer was found to have violated the National Labor Relations Act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: hearing...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: I mean, there was an administrative order to pay, wasn't there?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's what I thought.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: law judge did find that did not award any back pay to Castro because it conflicted with Sure-Tan had found that it conflicted with IRCA.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Th- fu- the but the board did.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: If he I guess you're quite right that if the if the board's award violates the immigration law, that's the end of the case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But suppose that the immigration law doesn't forbid that award in terms, but its policy would, in fact, be hindered, while the labor law, actually the policy is furthered, though it doesn't insist on this kind of award, wouldn't we defer to the board when it seeks to reconcile, or the Attorney General when they seek to reconcile the policies of the two statutes?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Because...

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: I I would I would respectfully disagree, and this in Sure-Tan this Court said in devising remedies for unfair labor practices the board is obliged to take into account another equally important congressional objective, to wit, the objective of deterring unauthorized immigration that is embodied in the INA.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, that goes back as far as Southern Steamship Company, where you're talking about the mutiny statute, that the board can't just go ahead without any reference to competing statutes.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I would agree, if if the board's remedy conflicts with the policies of another statutory scheme, then it it's they're owed no deference.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: The board did take that into account, though.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: The APRA Fuel case?

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and that a pr-

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: go back to one thing it also said in Sure-Tan, which is that the court of appeals here made an estimate without any evidence as to the period of time these employees might have continued working before apprehension by the INS, and that sounds working before apprehension by the INS, that sounds very close to what the board did in this case?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, also in Sure-Tan, in the last sentence of the remedial section of the opinion it states, by directing the board to impose a minimum back pay award without regard to the employee's actual economic losses or legal availability to work, the court of appeals plainly exceeded its limited authority under the act.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: So then, th- what what do you make of the the condition about the period between the violation and when the employees might have continued working before apprehension by the INS?

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: I Your Honor, I don't know, because the discriminatees in that case were left the country the same day...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: their employment terminated.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: the outcome of that particular case, where they were in Mexico, but a case where they were here, and the question was, how much back pay, and there's a reference to not a flat six weeks or six months or whatever, but the time period from the unlawful employment practice until one would expect the INS would pick up these people.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Your Honor, again I think that that is inconsistent with the other language in the Sure-Tan decision which is repeated over and over again about legal availability to work, and being legally authorized to work, and why would the Court use that language if it didn't have to? So I getting back to the policy of this that underlies the immigration laws of this country, is to preserve jobs for American workers.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. McCortney.

turns.name: RYAN D. MCCORTNEY
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Wolfson before you get to the substance of the matter I I want to ask a question sort of relates to something that Justice Stevens asked Stevens asked.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in this matter?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: when when when it was told that that it want that that you're you're going to argue that courts should pay illegal aliens money that it was unlawful for them to earn? What what did the INS say to that? breath>

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Justice Scalia, may it please the Court: the the position in our brief has been developed in consultation with the Immigration and Naturalization Service.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: The INS has agreed with it and accepts it, and I'm...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: here representing -- Right right. Well, I...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, I mean, if i- if they agreed with it, I mean, and just thought...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The INS...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: i- I have no I it explains why we have a massive problem of illegal immigration, if that's if that's how the INS feels about this.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I have I I must disagree with that, Justice Scalia, and the point is, as the board recognized both in this decision and in the APRA Fuel decision which preceded it, the essential problem is that there is, as the board pointed out, an inseverable connection between illegal immigration, the availability of jobs, and poor working conditions for employees for i- that illegal immigrants are wiling to take in the United States.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: What it could...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: in IRCA didn't the legisla- history of the report say that IRCA is not meant to undermine or diminish in any way labor protections in existing law, or to limit the powers of labor relations board to remedy unfair practices committed against undocumented employees?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: That is that is exactly right, so...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: What is exactly right, that's what a legislative...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: history...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I think it is important also to note that in IRCA Congress also wrote into law authorization of increased resources for enforcement of of Federal labor laws by the Department of Labor by the Wage and Hour Division and the Office of Federal Contract Compliance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in order --

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: it include the National Labor...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Relations Board?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: it did not refer to the National Labor Relations Board, but the point is, Congress knew that the Department of Labor sh- was enforcing and should enforce Federal labor laws at the behest of and for the benefit of illegal aliens to obtain back pay for them.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: They wanted to be sure that that aliens who had no right to be here, had no right to be earning any money...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: should should get paid for periods that they didn't work? It It's not a matter of their having worked and and and provided the employer with with a benefit, which you're then reimbursing them for, but they haven't worked.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: It it's n- it it is it does not just periods for which they have worked, which might arguably distinguish the Fair Labor...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Standards Act...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE

turns.text: Even though it's unlawful for them...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: even though it is.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I In In most back pay situations where the employer has committed an unfair labor practice and dismisses an employee improperly, the amount he's going to be stuck with for back pay is limited by the fact that the person unlawfully fired has to mitigate.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Scalia. Here's...

turns.text: w- here's a I wouldn't say that the unlaw- I wouldn't say that the undocumented alien has a duty to mitigate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: is not I...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I I I will agree with that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE

turns.text: consistent with the whole...

turns.text: need not do that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Not nece-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: home and eat chocolates and get my back pay...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Is it consistent with the --

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: d- does the does the board have to reconcile the Fair Labor Standards Act and the Office of Contract Compliance? I would think that it's responsibility as the National Labor Relations Act and the Immigration Act in this case.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: That is correct, Mr. Chief Justice, but I but the board did look to IRCA and the Court can look to IRCA, which includes these other provisions in the Department of Labor, to determine what Congress was attempting to achieve in IRCA, and those are, I think, reasonable indicators of what Congress' objectives were and how far it intended to go along with the legislative...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: history  just...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: makes makes its calculus and when the Government made its calculus, did it give any consideration to the fact that a union ought not as a matter of policy to use illegal aliens for organizing activity, or do you think the union can do that?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: consistent with the labor laws of the United States for the union say it knowingly uses an alien for organizing activity?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I I don't know that the board has addressed the point of knowingly using illegal aliens.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: they weren't...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: and that doe- that doesn't d- in- induce illegal immigration?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well,

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: It seems to me that's a far more direct link than the very tenuous idea that you have that there's going to be more illegal immigration because they know they're going to get back pay.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, I don't know I would suggest that the the more powerful inducement towards illegal migration is the availability of the job, union or no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: is the employer--

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: cases we're only talking about a situation where the employer, and presumably the union, too, doesn't know, because if if the employer knows, then the employer's obligation is to dismiss that person, as I assume it would be the union's, too, but in all these cases the issue comes up only because the employer didn't know of the illegality, isn't that so?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, in this case the employer did not know.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But not back pay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I mean...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: does order back pay up to the point where up to the point where either the employee is reinstated, or it terminates at a reasonable i- if the employee fails to put himself in compliance with the immigration laws and become qualified, the board cuts off back pay as of that As of the end of a reasonable period.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And that is consistent...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA

turns.text: Well, that is because in a case like this, what the what the board is doing is affording the employer the benefit of its general,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: he knows about illegality from the very beginning, then we will...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, in...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: a situ- in a situation...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: order back pay?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Yes, because in a situation like that the employee the employer is not well-situated to claim the benefit of the after-acquired evidence rule, because it cannot claim that it wouldn't have hired the alien...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: anyway t- t- to use an example.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there are two people violating...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: the law here, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: properly, sir.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: of the initial hiring up...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: until his...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: violation.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, the board is the board is charged with the responsibility of of developing a workable implementation of the National Labor Relations Act pursuant to its authority under section ten C.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: law, but there is no...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: doing though, really is kind of odd, because the the result is that back pay awards to illegal workers are likely to be greater than to legal ones under this board's policy, and that's so odd, and it gives the illegal alien an incentive to try to phony up more documents and to extend for the longest possible time the charade that the worker is here lawfully, and that's that's surely strongly against the policies of the immigration act at the very least.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: The the problem, Justice O'Connor, is that the immigration act, the policies come down on both sides.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, but you y-

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: You say it comes down on both sides, and it it makes certain acts by illegal immigrants illegal, the even the entry and it makes certain acts by the employer illegal.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: That is correct, Mr. Chief Justice, but the board is attempting to fashion the board doesn't know in advance which employer will violate the law by hiring undocumented aliens knowingly and which will not, or which employer will,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: you know posel- I  I...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I think that the that the rule the board isn't is fashioning a rule that is implementing Congress' policy...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: objective er i- across the board...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: be looked at after the fact, let's find out who violated what and then fashion the appropriate remedy?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, the Court has not said that the board has to only fashion its remedies in a case in which the precise situation in the pr- e- i- you know in the precise situation into which it's it's dedicated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: large...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: prosecution for viola- if if they know that it's it's an illegal...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: It's not just the problem of their knowing of their knowing that they're illegal hiring.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well well it's just not reasonable to...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Why should why should the board be responsible for making sure that the employer documents his material about illegal aliens?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I I don't think that it's not that the board is implementing IRCA, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: discussing. Th-

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: here's the thing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: None at all. If...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: is an incentive, it seems to me, for the employee to continue to conceal the facts, the facts that he's here illegally and has no right to work, and that the documents are false, and the board's rule fosters both those...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: But one does have to take into account I think what would be the consequence of an opposite rule.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But why should they be used tif- to-  to organize a bargaining unit...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: if they're ill if they're illegal employees? I'm baffled by that.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Because it well, because the labor laws benefit everybody, and it's not just I mean, they are when I say they're organizing a bargaining unit, obviously there are there may be in this case there were other citizens and authorized aliens who are interested in organizing for the purpose of vindicating their rights under section seven of the Labor Relations act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: You see...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I would have thought, I would have thought Mr. Wolfson, that when you said, you know, there are seven million illegal aliens in this country, that what you would follow that with is not, that's an awful lot of people not to give back pay to.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And what...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And what you don't want to do to reduce it is to give them back pay.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Now, there is no question that there is a serious problem of illegal immigration, and the INS and the Attorney General dedicate substantial resources to attempting to resolve that problem.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: another worker.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Mr. Wolfson the ar- if every time-- And I I understand that argument.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I I wouldn't say that the board has limited its it certainly hasn't limited its remedy to that situation...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: after the comment because...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: why in the question-- because I have exactly the same question.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: W- we are not suggesting that the Court has to decide that case here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: fuel program but but...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Souter said I had taken to be the precise rationale for the narrow set of cases we're supposed to to d- decide.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Clearly you haven't missed your opportunity.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Justice Bergam, [: Breyer] my point is that the board in a case like the board did rely on its APRA Fuel decision in this case, but what it it held in this case was that an employer, once an employer learns that an alien is undocumented and therefore he cannot be hired, and in addition that the board accepts that that employer would not have would not have hired that alien and would have discharged the alien when it found out that he was undocumented, then the employer should surely get the benefit of the after-acquired evidence rule that is applied in m- many, many kinds of Federal labor laws, including the Age Discrimination Act, for example.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: On- once you adopt that rule, however, it seems to me that the asserted benefit to the immigration laws that you're claiming this rule has disappears.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: You're you're...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: trying to deter the employer who hires an immigrant apparently with with this in the back of his mind, I know this guy's an immigrant and I can commit an im- unfair labor practice upon him.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to people who don't know.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: whether or not he may not know in the face of having clear, irrefutable evidence before him.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: the verification system. Because because...

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, that of course, that's not...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But but your isn't isn't...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: another way to describe the class that you're dealing with is, he may not know in the sense that the Government can prove...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: he knew, but there is a class of employers as to whom the evidence is not clear, and it is that class that we want to provide the negative inducement for.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: answer?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I think at at a m- I I would say at a minimum that is the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: answer, and the board and the board cannot know in advance--

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Why should the board take over responsibility for those provisions that are basically immigration law provisions?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, the board does not view itself as enforcing the immigration laws.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What you just agreed to in your colloquy with Justice Souter does contradict the the the immigration I I I remember when this statute was passed about what the responsibility of the employer was.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: The- They don't have any liability under IRCA.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: or to...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: more than what the statute requires.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I I do want to I do want to touch a bit on Sure-Tan, which I haven't had much of a chance to discuss.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Wolfson, do do you think a State court could enforce a contract for employment by an illegal alien?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I do not think that a State court...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: could r- could Right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: contract with an employer then it it is he sues because the employer doesn't go ahead with it, and and could a State court say, well, you made the contract, you should be liable for damages?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I I think a State court could award damages, and State courts have done so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: have...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: doctrine that that contracts in violation of the law are unenforceable?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Th- they're not necessarily completely void.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But all of that is for work done?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: No.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: or injury suffered, not to enforce a contract which has not been executed, which is what's going on here.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, there may perhaps there is a difference between tort and contract damages, but it is not just for work done.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Wolfson.
